name: Richard Liaw
bio: Richard Liaw is a co-creator of Ray Tune and a Product leader at Anyscale, the company commercializing the Ray distributed computing framework. A UC Berkeley RISELab graduate researcher under Joseph Gonzalez, Ion Stoica, and Ken Goldberg, Liaw is a co-author of the foundational Ray paper (OSDI 2018, 1,560 citations), the RLlib reinforcement learning library (ICML, 1,006 citations), and the Tune hyperparameter tuning platform (1,079 citations). He co-authored 'Learning Ray' (O'Reilly, 2023) and has an h-index of 13 with over 4,300 total citations across 20 publications. His research spans distributed systems, hyperparameter optimization (HyperSched, RubberBand), and scalable reinforcement learning.

timeline.event: Co-authored 'Learning Ray: Flexible Distributed Python for Machine Learning' published by O'Reilly Media (with Max Pumperla and Edward Oakes)
Co-authored Anyscale blog post 'How to Distribute Hyperparameter Tuning Using Ray Tune'
Published 'RubberBand: Cloud-based Hyperparameter Tuning' at EuroSys 2021 (28 citations)
Joined Anyscale full-time as software engineer, transitioning from UC Berkeley PhD program
Interviewed on Software Engineering Daily about Ray applications, RLlib, and Tune
Presented 'The Road to AutoML: Hyperparameter Tuning and Neural Architecture Search' at Anyscale event
Published 'HyperSched: Dynamic Resource Reallocation for Model Development on a Deadline' at ACM SoCC 2019 (49 citations)
Presented at PyData Los Angeles 2019 on distributed hyperparameter tuning with Ray Tune
Co-authored 'Ray: A Distributed Framework for Emerging AI Applications' at OSDI 2018 (1,560 citations)
Published 'Tune: A Research Platform for Distributed Model Selection and Training' (1,079 citations), presented at ICML AutoML Workshop 2018
Co-authored 'RLlib: Abstractions for Distributed Reinforcement Learning' at ICML (1,006 citations)

key_contributions.title: Ray Tune
key_contributions.description: Co-created Tune, a unified framework for distributed hyperparameter tuning and model selection that supports ASHA, BOHB, PBT, and integrates with PyTorch, TensorFlow, XGBoost, and Keras. Over 1,079 citations.

key_contributions.title: Ray (OSDI 2018)
key_contributions.description: Co-authored the foundational Ray paper introducing a general-purpose distributed framework for emerging AI applications with fault-tolerant primitives. 1,560 citations.

key_contributions.title: RLlib
key_contributions.description: Co-authored the scalable reinforcement learning library providing composable abstractions for multi-agent environments and policy serving. 1,006 citations.

key_contributions.title: Learning Ray (O'Reilly, 2023)
key_contributions.description: Co-authored the definitive O'Reilly book on Ray with Max Pumperla and Edward Oakes, covering distributed Python for machine learning including training and cluster management.

key_contributions.title: HyperSched
key_contributions.description: Developed a dynamic application-level resource scheduler that reallocates resources to best-performing hyperparameter trials under deadline constraints. Published at ACM SoCC 2019.

key_contributions.title: RubberBand
key_contributions.description: Cloud-based hyperparameter tuning system for cost-efficient model selection, published at EuroSys 2021.

quotes.text: Ray is a compute engine for scaling AI workloads, offering libraries for training, serving, and data processing.
Developing locally and scaling into large-scale contexts without needing a distributed systems PhD was a key design principle.
Ray Data makes batch inference and image processing pipelines 100 times easier than forcing them into weird setups.
